topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of old paper and stale coffee suddenly felt suffocating. Leo froze mid-sentence, his hand hovering over his textbook. He’d just turned to grab his water bottle when he noticed it—a book lying open on his desk, the spine cracked just so. It was his journal. And the handwriting inside wasn't his. It was neat, elegant, and chillingly familiar. A cold dread washed over him, tightening his chest until it was hard to draw a full breath. He slowly lowered his hand, his eyes darting from the page to his roommate, Maya, who was engrossed in her laptop across the room, a faint, satisfied smile playing on her lips. Every muscle in Leo’s body seemed to lock up. He felt a sudden, intense urge to bolt, to vanish into the hallway, but his feet remained rooted to the floor.

[story 2]

"Hey, did you see my blue pen?" Leo asked, trying to sound casual, but his voice came out thin and reedy. He looked up from his notes, and his gaze snagged on the open notebook resting beside his monitor. It was his private diary. The ink flowed across the page, detailing conversations and anxieties he hadn't even thought to write down yet. A wave of nausea rolled through him, making his stomach churn violently. He could feel the blood draining from his face, leaving his skin clammy and cold. He swallowed hard, the sound loud in the quiet dorm room. He couldn't look away; the meticulous script seemed to mock him, revealing secrets he hadn't intended to share.

[story 3]

I walked into the room expecting to find my notes scattered, but instead, I found my journal open. It wasn't just open; it was actively being read. Maya was sitting on the edge of her bed, her head tilted, her eyes tracing the lines of my recent entries with an unnerving stillness. My breath hitched in my throat. I took an involuntary step backward, bumping against the doorframe. My hands started shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the desk for support. The air in the room seemed to thicken, pressing down on my lungs. When she finally looked up, her expression was blank, utterly devoid of recognition, and that was when I knew everything was irrevocably wrong.
